speaker: interviewer
text: --our interview with Clara Heller Isaacman. You said that you came in 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel to get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go from Belgium to France. And we took a ship out-- at Le Havre. And the ship was an American boat that had 800 war brides. And it was very fascinating that most of the war brides really wanted to see America and didn't know who they married or what they married. And a lot-- many of them went back. Quite a few of us, I hope-- and myself included-- we knew who we married and who we-- what became. The, the trip was a very fascinating trip because I learned a lot about life of the years that I wasn't around, that I didn't exist. Because I felt I was reborn after the war. And the ship gave me a lot of insight on the people, on their values, on their understanding, and also even on the war. They had gone through Second World War. And they didn't realize what they went through. And I came over with that ship. I will show the articles on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said that you have been back to, to Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have been back to Antwerp many times. First of all, the-- a, a few times because my book was translated in Dutch. And they're using it in Belgium. And I had to go back for that. But then I also went back to see those who are still alive and who saved my life. And I, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I enjoyed it. I remained friends with them. Most of them don't want to be recognized per se. They don't want a tree here or something there. They did it because they did it. And they remain my friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Too many of my Jewish friends I didn't find. Most of them perished in camps, and God only knows where else.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back with my mother to rebury my father, when I told the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had your mother come here to live at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother lived in upstate New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed, I think, 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find much changed from after the war as compared to before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the-- first of all, the Jews in Antwerp now are not Belgium Jews, like they-- you know. There are Jews who came from Israel who live there now, and Jews who came from other places of the world. Very few real Antwerp Jews live in Antwerp. There are [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] two synagogues and one very Orthodox school. The sad thing, when I went to shul-- to synagogue there on Saturday, there is guards on the synagogue. You have to-- they have to know you to go in because they are afraid for anti-Semitism, for a bomb, for something else, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the synagogue is protected, what I felt very ill at ease. I went into that synagogue before the war. And nobody at the door asked me who I am. And here, I had to show my passport and say, I'm a visitor from America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody there recognized me. They knew anyway who I am. But there is a lot of changes there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was the same synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you doing now? What have you been doing the past many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I try to be a good mother to my son. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I try to be very busy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with things that I feel that when I'm gone I will leave something of value. And that-- I don't mean money. I am writing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've written two books. And I'm writing another one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I'm writing two. One is a children's book. And one is an adult book. And the children book is a Haggadah for Passover on the Holocaust and on the Haggadah. And I'm writing it together with a rabbi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I teach what I-- it's my life. I love to stand in front of a class. I love students. There's not a student that I do not find a challenge in. So I love to teach. I lecture a lot. I've lectured at the Washington Museum, lectured in many states in the United States of America. And I give seminars to schools. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The rest, what I like to do is sit Saturday afternoon on my chair and listen to the opera. When I'm not going now to the opera-- so the opera comes to my house. I, I have a few friends that we go sometimes to a good show, but not too often. I read a lot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Whatever there is new, I want to know. And I read a lot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I used to love gardening. But I don't have time for it. So I don't do it anymore. All my trees outside, everything that is around my house was planted by me. I loved it. My husband used to sit inside with this book and with his hi-fi. But I loved it. But now I feel-- it's not a waste of time. But I don't have time for both. So I can't do-- so I'm choosing what I'm doing. I don't go to many meetings. I don't belong to many organizations. I will support them, but not go because I don't have time. I don't-- I don't-- I can't think of anything else that I'm doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about a couple of the things that you've done in connection with the Shoah and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With the Shoah, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I really live my life, in part, on the Shoah because I feel that when I'm gone-- we survivors are gone, there will be a gap. Even if our children-- the best who understood us and who will read will still not feel what we felt and will not understand. So the Shoah and what is being done today here for me-- and I hope, for the future generation-- is something that has to be taught. And every little article or every lecture that I can do, that I can teach the younger generation-- I think that we have-- we missed a generation from teaching the Shoah, at least one. 20 years after the Holocaust, nobody was taught about the Shoah. Then it became a fashion. I hope it's not just a fashion. But I-- so I, I write a lot of articles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I teach at synagogue schools, particularly. I like to teach eight grades and up. And our children-- it's amazing. When I go into a public school, the teachers there, particularly not the Jewish teachers, think that our Jewish children know about the Shoah. But they don't. They're not being taught, maybe, in some schools, as much as in the public schools. And I'm trying very hard to spread the word, to make people understand that that's part of our history. It doesn't have to become our life. It's not mine neither-- all of it. I like a good football game. I will watch it on television. I like a-- I don't play golf. But I love nature. So I love to watch those golf clubs. And, and I know a little bit the rules, so-- but just to watch. What I'm trying to do is have a life, not a one particular thing, but live my life, a rich life for everything else. But the Shoah is my heart and my soul and my body. And it can't be different because I went through it. And what I'm trying real hard-- and whenever I speak about the Shoah, deep down in my heart and in my mind are all those people who cannot say a word-- my brother, my uncles, those that I know, that I knew. I'm not talking about the millions that I didn't know, but those hundreds that I knew that are no more there. I'm trying to make people understand that this shouldn't happen again. And that's what I'm doing for the Shoah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the lady in this picture, Mrs. Isaacman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Rose Halpert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was maybe 21 years old. And that's maybe 70 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my two brothers, my sister, and myself. The smallest one is Elie Heller, Frieda Heller, Heshie Heller, and Clara Heller. The picture was taken approximately in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belgium, Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these two girls are?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I. The smaller one is Frieda Heller-- Clara Heller. And this was taken the same year, like in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Antwerp, Belgium. This is the last picture that was taken of my whole family together. And it was taken in 1941. The first, near my father, having my hand, is me, Clara Heller. Then it's my daddy, Sholom Heller, Elie Heller, Rose Heller, Heshie Heller, and Frieda Heller. It was taken in Antwerp. [SIGHS] This is the last picture of my brother that-- when we ever saw him. And it's Heshie Heller.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Antwerp in 1941. My father, Sholom Heller, taken in Antwerp, Belgium, in 1941. My mother's only sister, Esther Halpert. I don't know when it was taken. But I know it was taken in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother's brothers, taken in Budapest. I don't know the year. Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Emil, and one was Baruch-- with their wives. I know it was taken before the war, so it could be in 1940 or 1939. This is another brother of my mother with-- mother's-- with his wife. I don't even know his name. It's a Halpert. That's all I can tell you about this picture. This is Zigo Halpert with his wife. He survived the Holocaust. His wife and children were killed in Auschwitz. This is his second wife. And this picture was taken in 1962 in Netanya, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is he related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother, my uncle. [SIGHS] This is a cousin of mine, one of my mother's brother's children. I don't know more about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: My grandfather, my mother's father, Heshie Halpert. Do you know where that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you have any idea of when? [LAUGHS] [LAUGHS] My mother told me, if I recall right, when she gave me this picture and the other one, that she was a young child. So 1800-something? Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, Rivka Halpert. The same thing-- those two pictures were taken in the same years. When, I don't know. This is Emil Halpert with his wife and his daughter in Ramat HaSharon in Israel. He gave it to me in 1969.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the, the ladies' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Elie with his girlfriend in camp, just before the war. I don't know his girlfriend's name. Belgium, Antwerp-- or not exactly in Antwerp, in a little town in Antwerp. The third one from my left is my brother, Heshie, with his friends. This was taken in camp two or three years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the country--

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: --inside of Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the-- in the country inside of Belgium, definitely. My sister at a farm, also in camp in the country outside of Antwerp. This picture are my friends of Hashomer Hatzair the last year we went to camp. And they all perished. There isn't one alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is in [NON-ENGLISH] Park in Antwerp. And I am-- [LAUGHS] my leader is right near me. And we are playing games. And we, we took this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you point out which one are you? Or is it too difficult in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's right here. Oh, no, it's not.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe where you are, which person it is, for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- from the right, the first girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the first girl on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and about when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taking-- taken in 1940. The same here-- Hashomer Hatzair. And I'm there. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You want me to point it out? I can-- Was it a special occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We used to march and sing on the streets of Antwerp. And this is one of the, the-- going to a park, marching and singing in the streets of Antwerp. I spoke about non-Jewish friends. Those were a group. I am the last one to the right. The rest were all my friends, but non-Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1940. Yeah, it would be '40. All of those perished. This is another camp that we were-- what I was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Belgium as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Belgium, yes, outside of Antwerp someplace. [SIGHS] Those were my brother's friends. I don't know their names. This was taken-- as you see, it's winter. And they-- it could've been even in the backyard of our home-- having fun and playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when do you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately-- I think it was the year he-- in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were my sister's friends. All of them perished. And this was taken in 1938. And I know why I remember it so, because it was one of her birthdays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my best friends, Elaine.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a blank.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and that was about when? Was it before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. It's before the war. I just don't remember the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, do you remember what happened? Do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she, she died. She was-- she perished in Auschwitz. Her name is there. Those were my Hashomer Hatzair scout leaders. Not one of them remained alive. This was taken in a park in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was [NON-ENGLISH]. One was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know the other ones. My sister is on the left, kneeling, with her best friends and her group. And this was taken in Antwerp. I remember her-- when she gave it to me. It was in 1938. Also, my sister used to love to give me pictures when her birthday was. And that's-- I should put in my album her friends' birthdays pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of any of the other people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, not now. This is the same picture. And you can see my sister standing-- where's the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't do it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's there. Very-- they put her in the middle. This was taken in 1941. Those were my two best friends. And that's how I saw them last. I never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Rachel and Sara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was [? Berkowitz. ?] And one was-- I don't remember. [SIGHS] I don't remember. The [? Berkowitz ?] I remember on the right-hand side. Those were a group of my school friends. And it was taken in the backyard of my Hebrew school. When I told you the story about the truck, part of them were in the truck, in that truck. The rest of them perished, too, but later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anybody there whose name that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Rebeka. She was the fourth one from my left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- blank.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This gentleman is the Jew who-- oh, I have his name right there. Do you remember his name? I, I wrote it down. It's there someplace. I really don't remember. I'm sorry. I have a blank right now. I guess I'm tired. He saved quite a few Jews. And then he was caught in Antwerp and shot in Antwerp by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- in a park-- Buchalter. [SIGHS] A group of my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do we have any names to go with this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is before the war as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --absolutely. All of those pictures are before the war. I mean, I don't remember any that I have here that are after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is after the war. This is a citation-- a, a slogan, not a citation, from the Belgium king to one of the resistant people who fell who helped Jews. And it says, 'In memory of all of those who fought for liberty, for righteousness.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the purpose of that? What was the occasion for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave that for a lot of-- a lot of Undergrounds, people who worked for the Underground. When they died-- I'm sorry. Thank you. When they died, then they, they gave it to their parents for a memory, whatever. This was one of the people that I saw maybe twice during hiding. But I remember him very well. He was caught. He was killed. His parents gave me this picture when I was once in Antwerp. I went to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in hiding? Or was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he wasn't Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he was in the Resistance, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the Resistance. And he helped. And he was also helping Belgium. I mean, he worked a lot for the Resistance. And when I went to visit his parents, they gave me this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say his name aloud? Bosmans? Rene Bosmans.
